analogy: Think of "Mineral and Energy Resources" through the chapter's own opening images: Unit IV Unit IV Unit IV Unit IV Unit IV Chapter 7 TRANSPORT AND COMMUNICATION We use many items in our daily life. From toothpaste to our bed tea, milk, clothes, soaps, food items, etc., are required every day. Have you ever thought as to... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: TRANSPORT AND, COMMUNICATION, 76 India : People and Economy, 78 India : People and Economy. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Mineral and Energy Resources is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that unit IV Unit IV Unit IV Unit IV Unit IV Chapter 7 TRANSPORT AND COMMUNICATION We use many items in our daily life.

The text explains that from toothpaste to our bed tea, milk, clothes, soaps, food items, etc., are required every day.

The text explains that have you ever thought as to how these items are brought from the site of production?

The text explains that from the fields and factory, the produce is brought to the place from where consumers purchase it.

The text explains that it is the transportation of these items from the site of their production to the market which make them available to the consumer.

The text explains that we not only use material things, like fruits, vegetables, books, clothes, etc., but also use ideas, views and messages in our daily life.

The text explains that do you know we exchange our views, ideas and messages from one place to another or one individual to another while communicating with the help of various means?

The text explains that the use of transport and communication depends upon our need to move things from place of their availability to the place of their use.

The text explains that human beings use various methods to move goods, commodities, ideas from one place to another.

The text explains that the following diagram shows the major means of transportation.

The text explains that land T Land T Land T Land T Land Trrrrranspor anspor anspor anspor ansporttttt The pathways and unmetalled roads have been used for transportation in India since ancient times.

The text explains that with the economic and technological development, metalled roads and railways were developed to move large volume of goods and

The text explains that 76 India : People and Economy people from one place to another.

The text explains that ropeways, cableways and pipelines were devised to cater to the demands of transporting specific goods under special circumstances.

The text explains that road Transport India has one of the second largest road networks in the world with a total length of about 62.16 lakh km (morth.nic.in, Annual Report 2020-21). the princely states and British India.

The text explains that after Independence, twenty-year road plan (1961) was introduced to improve the conditions of roads in India.

The text explains that national Highways The main roads which are constructed and maintained by the Central Government are known as the National Highways.

The text explains that other Roads 5902539 Total 6215797 Table 7.1 : India Road Network 2020 National Highways Development Projects NHAI has taken up some major projects in the country under different phases : Golden Quadrilateral : It comprises construction of 5,846-km long 4/6 lane, high density traffic corridor, to connect India’s four big metro cities of Delhi-Mumbai-Chennai- Kolkata.

The text explains that there is regional variation in the density of rural road because these are influenced by the nature of the terrain.

The text explains that state Highways These are constructed and maintained by state governments.

The text explains that 7.2 : Road constructed under the Pradhan Mantri Gram Sadak Yojna Why are the rural roads’ density very low in hilly, plateau and forested areas?

The text explains that source: Ministry of Road Transport and Highways Annual Report 2020-21.

The text explains that 78 India : People and Economy northern and north-eastern boundary of the country.

The text explains that source: http://www.bro.gov.in/ pagefimg.asp?imid=144, And PIB Delhi 03 October 2020

The text explains that bharatmala Pariyojana – ‘Road’ to country’s infrastructure development • The Bharatmala Pariyojna envisages development of about 26,000 km length of Economic Corridors, which along with Golden Quadrilateral (GQ) and North–South and East–West (NS-EW) Corridors are expected to carry majority of freight trafiic on roads. • The Programme focuses on development of ring roads, bypasses and elevated corridores to decongest the traffic passing through cities and enhance logistic efficiency. [Source: Press Information Bureau (Reseach Unit), Ministry of Information and Broadcasting, Government of India, December 13, 2021] Rail Transport Indian Railways, network is one of the longest in the world.

The text explains that it facilitates the movement of both freight and passengers and contributes to the growth of the economy.

The text explains that indian Railways is the largest government undertaking in the country.

The text explains that its very large size puts a lot of pressure on a centralised railway management system.

The text explains that thus, in India, the railway system has been divided into 17 zones.

The text explains that metro rail has revolutionlised the urban transport system in India.

The text explains that these were mostly developed for the exploitation of resources.

The text explains that the most significant development has been the development of Konkan Railway along the western coast providing a direct link between Mumbai and Mangaluru.

The text explains that (Source: Annual Report, Ministry of Ports, Shipping & Waterways, Govt. of India, 2022-23)

The text explains that 82 India : People and Economy The Inland Waterways Authority has also identified 10 other inland waterways, which could be upgraded.

The text explains that oceanic routes play an important role in the transport sector of India’s economy.

The text explains that gAIL (India) Ltd. was set up in 1984 as a public sector undertaking to transport, process and market natural gas for its economic use.

The text explains that overall, India’s gas infrastructure has expanded over ten times from 1,700 km to 18,500 km of cross-country pipelines and is expected to soon reach over 34,000 km as Gas Grid by linking all the gas sources and consuming markets across the country including North Eastern States.

The text explains that on the basis of scale and quality, the mode of communication can be divided into following categories : Personal Communication System Among all the personal communication system internet is the most effective and advanced one.

The text explains that it enables the user to establish direct contact through e-mail to get access to the world of knowledge and information.

The text explains that it enables us with the basic facilities of direct communication.

The text explains that mass Communication System Radio Radio broadcasting started in India in 1923 by the Radio Club of Bombay.

The text explains that government took this opportunity and brought this popular mode of communication under its control in 1930 under the Indian Broadcasting System.

The text explains that 84 India : People and Economy EXERCISES EXERCISES EXERCISES EXERCISES EXERCISES 1.

The text explains that choose the right answers of the following from the given options.

The text explains that (i) In how many zones has the Indian Railways system been divided?

The text explains that however, use of satellite in getting a continuous and synoptic view of larger area has made satellite communication very vital for the country due to the economic and strategic reasons.

The text explains that on the basis of configuration and purposes, satellite system in India can be grouped into two: Indian National Satellite System (INSAT) and Indian Remote Sensing Satellite System (IRS).

The text explains that the INSAT, which was established in 1983,is a multi-purpose satellite system for telecommunication, meteorological observation and for various other data and programmes.

The text explains that the IRS satellite system became operational with the launching of IRS-IA in March 1988 from Vaikanour in Russia.

The text explains that the National Remote Sensing Centre (NRSC) at Hyderabad provides facilities for acquisition of data and its processing.

The text explains that india has also developed her own Launching Vehicle PSLV (Polar Satellite Launch Vehicle).

The text explains that these satellites collect data in several spectral bands and transmit them to the ground stations for various uses.

Source: NCERT Class 12 India People and Economy, Chapter 7: Mineral and Energy Resources
